bio_en: Amr ibn al-As (RA) — this entry 'Amr' primarily refers to the famous companion and general Amr ibn al-As al-Sahmi al-Qurashi, though the name was common among multiple narrators. Amr ibn al-As was one of the most brilliant military minds of early Islam: he embraced Islam in 8 AH, shortly before the conquest of Mecca, and was immediately entrusted by the Prophet ﷺ with military and diplomatic missions. He led the conquest of Egypt in 19–20 AH under Caliph Umar (RA). He narrated approximately 39 hadiths. He was known for his sharp intellect, strategic thinking, and, in his later years, deep personal piety. He reportedly said on his deathbed that he had passed through three states: unbelief, Islam without full sincerity, and finally true sincere submission to Allah. He passed away in Egypt around 42–43 AH as its governor.

Al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba al-Thaqafi (RA) was from the tribe of Thaqif in Ta'if and embraced Islam before the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah in 6 AH. He was one of the most politically astute companions, serving as a diplomat, military commander, and governor under multiple caliphs. He served as governor of Basra under Umar (RA) and later as governor of Kufa under Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan, a post he held until his death. He was renowned for his wit and intelligence, with many witty exchanges attributed to him in classical literature. He narrated approximately 136 hadiths on various topics including prayer, contracts, and prophetic conduct. He performed Hajj with the Prophet ﷺ, participated in many campaigns, and was involved in the conquest of Iraq and Persia. He passed away in Kufa around 50 AH.

Abu Salama ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf was a leading Tabi'i scholar of Medina and is counted among the Seven Jurists of Medina (al-fuqaha al-sab'a). He was the son of the companion Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf (RA) — one of the ten companions promised Paradise — which gave him access to some of the most authoritative companions from his earliest years. He narrated extensively from Aisha (RA), Abu Huraira (RA), and other senior companions. His relationship with Abu Huraira was particularly close and productive, and he transmitted a large portion of Abu Huraira's narrations. He was regarded by hadith critics as fully trustworthy and was one of the most important transmitters in the Medinan scholarly tradition. Imam al-Zuhri narrated from him extensively. He passed away around 94 AH.

Rafi ibn Khadij al-Ansari (RA) was from the Ansar of Medina, from the tribe of Aws. He was one of the companions who was initially turned away from the Battle of Badr for being too young, but he participated in subsequent campaigns including Uhud and Khandaq. He narrated approximately 78 hadiths. He is most well known for his narrations on agricultural contracts (muzara'a and mukhabara) — the Prophet ﷺ forbade certain share-cropping arrangements that might lead to injustice, and Rafi's narrations on this topic are the primary source for the relevant legal rulings in Islamic jurisprudence. He also narrated hadiths on hunting and permissible slaughter. He settled in Medina after the Prophet's death and was a respected narrator there. He passed away around 73–74 AH.

Abu Ishaq al-Sabi'i, whose full name was Amr ibn Abdullah ibn Ubaid al-Hamdani al-Sabi'i, was a prominent Tabi'i scholar from Kufa. He narrated from a large number of companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib, al-Bara ibn Azib, Zaid ibn Arqam, and Hubaysh ibn Junadah. He was considered highly reliable (thiqah) by hadith critics in his earlier years of narration. He was also a significant authority in Quranic recitation and Islamic law. In his later years some critics noted that his memory may have been affected, and a small number of later narrations attributed to him were treated with additional scrutiny. He was the teacher of Sufyan al-Thawri, Shu'ba ibn al-Hajjaj, and other leading scholars of the following generation. He passed away in Kufa around 127–129 AH.

Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (RA) — this entry 'Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (RA)' appearing in the Bulugh al-Maram collection refers once again to Sa'd ibn Malik ibn Sinan al-Ansari, the prolific Ansari companion documented under entries 11, 15, 17, 59, and 79. He participated in twelve battles with the Prophet ﷺ, narrated over 1,170 hadiths, and was a resident of Medina until his death around 74 AH. His narrations in Bulugh al-Maram reflect his broad legal knowledge and his eyewitness proximity to the Prophet ﷺ.

Tawus ibn Kaysan al-Yamani al-Hamdani was a leading Tabi'i scholar and jurist from Yemen. He was born around 33 AH in Yemen and narrated from a wide range of companions including Abdullah ibn Abbas, Abdullah ibn Umar, Abu Huraira, Zayd ibn Thabit, and Jabir ibn Abdullah (may Allah be pleased with them all). He was considered one of the most learned men of his generation, combining mastery of hadith with deep legal acumen. He was famous for his independence: he repeatedly refused gifts and financial support from rulers and governors, and famously refused to stand up for Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik (the Umayyad Caliph) at Hajj, which caused a commotion. He was known for his serious demeanor and profound asceticism. He passed away in Mecca around 106 AH during the Hajj season and was buried there.

Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan (RA) was the son of Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, the Qurayshi leader who opposed Islam for many years before embracing it. Muawiya himself accepted Islam at the Conquest of Mecca in 8 AH and served as a scribe recording the Quranic revelations — a position of great trust near the end of the Prophet's life. He narrated approximately 163 hadiths. He became the governor of Syria under Caliph Umar (RA) and later under Uthman (RA). After the civil war following Uthman's assassination, he became the founder of the Umayyad Caliphate and ruled for nearly twenty years (41–60 AH). Ahl us-Sunnah regards him as a companion whose political role is accepted as part of the turbulent realities of early Islamic history, without engaging in cursing or condemnation. He passed away in Damascus in 60 AH.

Zaid ibn Khalid al-Juhani (RA) was from the tribe of Juhayna and was a companion of the Prophet ﷺ who participated in the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah in 6 AH. He was known for his piety and devotion to worship — there is a report that he observed the Prophet ﷺ leading the dawn prayer after a night of rain at Hudaybiyyah and then addressing the people about the spiritual states of those who attributed the rain to Allah versus those who attributed it to the stars. This narration is one of his most frequently cited. He narrated approximately 81 hadiths on topics including found property (luqata), prayer, and purification. He settled in Medina after the Prophet's death. He passed away around 68 AH.

Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam al-Asadi al-Qurashi (RA) was born in Medina in 1 AH — the first child born to the Muslim emigrants (Muhajirun) after the Hijra, and his birth was considered a blessed sign since the Jews of Medina had spread a rumor that they had bewitched the Muslims so they would have no children. The Prophet ﷺ performed the tahnik (softening a date and touching it to the newborn's palate) for him and named him. He was the son of al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam (RA) and the grandson of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (RA) through his mother Asma. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet ﷺ and from senior companions including Umar (RA) and Uthman (RA). He later became the Caliph in Mecca after refusing to pledge allegiance to Yazid ibn Muawiya, and he ruled from 64 AH until he was besieged and martyred by al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf in 73 AH.

Al-Nu'man ibn Bashir al-Ansari (RA) was born in Medina in 2 AH, making him one of the youngest companions. His father Bashir ibn Sa'd was also a companion, and his mother Amra bint Rawaha was the sister of the poet-companion Abdullah ibn Rawaha. When he was a few years old his mother brought him to the Prophet ﷺ, intending to make a gift of some property to him — the Prophet ﷺ asked his father whether he had given all his children the same gift, and when the answer was no, the Prophet ﷺ declined to be a witness to an unequal bequest, providing the basis for one of the most important legal principles on equal treatment of children. He narrated approximately 114 hadiths. He served as governor of Kufa and Homs under Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan. He was martyred around 65 AH during the period of strife after Yazid's death.

Abu Hazim al-Ashja'i was a Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths from several companions. The name Abu Hazim was borne by two notable Tabi'i scholars. The first, Salmaan — freed slave of Azda — narrated from companions in Kufa and the Hijaz and was generally regarded as reliable. The second and better-known scholar by this name was Abu Hazim Salama ibn Dinar al-Araj al-Madani, a Tabi'i of Medina who died around 135 AH. He was particularly notable for narrating extensively from Abu Huraira (RA) and Sahl ibn Sa'd (RA), and served as the teacher of Imam Malik ibn Anas. He was known for his deep asceticism, his philosophical sayings on life and death, and his frank speech with rulers. Both narrators sharing this name were trustworthy transmitters whose reports appear in the Sahihayn and Sunan collections.

Salama ibn al-Akwa al-Aslami (RA), whose full name was Salama ibn Amr ibn al-Akwa, was from the tribe of Aslam and was renowned among the companions for his exceptional speed in running on foot — a speed that reportedly allowed him to keep pace with a horse. He participated in many military expeditions, including Hudaybiyyah, where he gave his pledge under the tree. He was at Khaybar, Hunayn, and other major campaigns. He narrated approximately 77 hadiths, many relating to warfare, hunting, and the Prophet's conduct during expeditions. He is known for hadiths describing the pursuit of raiders and his personal exploits in protecting the Muslim community. In his later years he withdrew from the main population centers and settled in the Rabadha area, preferring a simpler life. He passed away around 74 AH.

Abu Huraira (RA) — this entry 'Abu Huraira (rad)' is yet another transliteration variant of the same most-prolific companion narrator, here appearing in the Bulugh al-Maram collection. His full biography is documented under entry 1. He narrated over 5,374 hadiths, served as governor of Bahrain, and taught thousands of students in Medina until his death around 57–59 AH. This designation 'rad' is an abbreviation of 'radiyallahu anhu' (may Allah be pleased with him).

Abu Bakra al-Thaqafi (RA) — this entry 'AbuBakrah' is an alternate transliteration of the same companion documented under entry 27, Nufay ibn al-Harith, who descended from the walls of Ta'if on a pulley and embraced Islam. He settled in Basra and became one of its most respected companions. He narrated approximately 132 hadiths and is particularly known for the narration forbidding participation in civil strife. He passed away in Basra around 51–52 AH.

Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA) — this entry 'Abdullah Ibn Abbas' represents the same companion as entries 5, 16, and 21, appearing in the Abu Dawud collection under this specific spelling. He was the cousin of the Prophet ﷺ, the foremost Quranic exegete among the companions, and a narrator of over 1,660 hadiths. The Prophet ﷺ prayed specifically for him to receive deep understanding of the religion. He passed away around 68 AH in Ta'if. His full biography is under entry 5.

Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri (Muhammad ibn Muslim ibn Shihab) — this entry represents the same Tabi'i scholar documented fully under entry 34, appearing here in Bukhari, Abu Dawud, and Tirmidhi under the shortened name 'Ibn Shihab.' He was one of the most important figures in the history of hadith compilation, the teacher of Imam Malik and many others, and a narrator from numerous companions. He passed away around 124 AH. His chains appear in virtually every chapter of the major collections.

Qatada ibn Di'ama al-Sadusi — this entry 'Qatadah' is an alternate transliteration of the same Tabi'i scholar documented under entry 55, appearing in Abu Dawud and Tirmidhi. He was the blind Basran scholar with an extraordinary memory who narrated from Anas ibn Malik and other companions. He was a student of al-Hasan al-Basri and a leading authority in tafsir and hadith. He passed away around 117–118 AH.

Abu Uthman al-Nahdi, whose full name was Abd al-Rahman ibn Mall (or Mihl) al-Nahdi, was a senior Tabi'i scholar from Basra who reportedly lived to an extraordinary age — some accounts say he reached 130 years of age. He narrated from several companions of the Prophet ﷺ including Abu Musa al-Ashari, Salman al-Farsi, Umar ibn al-Khattab, and Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf. He was considered fully reliable (thiqah) by all hadith critics, and his long life allowed him to serve as a direct witness to the companion generation and then transmit that knowledge well into the Tabi'i era. He was based in Basra. He passed away around 95–100 AH.

Salama — the name Salama was borne by several companions and Tabi'in. The most prominent is Salama ibn al-Akwa al-Aslami (RA), documented under entry 93, renowned for his speed in running and his participation in major military expeditions. Another is Salama ibn Kuhail al-Hadrami, a Tabi'i scholar of Kufa who narrated from companions and Successors and was considered reliable by critics, dying around 121–122 AH. Without further context, narrations attributed to 'Salama' in Bukhari likely refer to Salama ibn al-Akwa. Both narrators were trustworthy transmitters whose reports appear in the major collections.

Al-Sa'ib ibn Yazid al-Kindi (RA) was a companion of the Prophet ﷺ who, by his own account, was brought before the Prophet ﷺ as a child of seven years during the Farewell Pilgrimage, and the Prophet ﷺ passed his hand over his head in blessing. He participated in the Hajj al-Wada' as a young child, making him one of the few companions who could claim direct personal contact with the Prophet ﷺ from the very earliest age. He served in the administration during the caliphates of Umar (RA) and Uthman (RA). He narrated hadiths about the Prophet's physical appearance, his conduct in prayer, and accounts from the early community. He was reportedly one of the last surviving companions in Medina and lived to approximately 91 AH — dying around 91 or 92 AH.

Umm Atiyya al-Ansariyyah (RA), whose full name is given as Nusayba bint al-Harith or Nusayba bint Ka'b, was a female companion of the Prophet ﷺ from the Ansar of Medina. She was one of the women who participated in military expeditions, providing medical care, tending the wounded, and preparing food. She reportedly participated in seven expeditions alongside the Prophet ﷺ. She is particularly important for her narrations on the ritual washing of deceased women, the preparation of the female body for burial, and women's religious practice more generally. Her narrations on the Prophet's instructions for preparing the body of his daughter Zainab for burial are among the most detailed and important sources in Islamic law on funerary rites. She is one of the most frequently cited female companions in the hadith literature.

Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) — this entry 'Al-Bara bin Azib' in Tirmidhi and Bukhari is yet another transliteration of the same Ansari companion documented under entries 14, 38, 43, and 48. He participated in eighteen military expeditions, narrated approximately 305 hadiths, and settled in Kufa. His narrations cover prayer, descriptions of the Prophet, funeral rites, and battlefield accounts. He passed away around 72 AH.

Ibn Abi Mulaika — this refers to Abdullah ibn Ubaydullah ibn Abi Mulaika al-Tamimi al-Makki, a Tabi'i scholar and judge from Mecca. He served as a qadi (judge) during the caliphate of Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr and was held in high esteem for his legal knowledge and moral integrity. He narrated from a wide range of senior companions including Aisha (RA), from whom he heard many narrations directly, as well as from Ibn Abbas, Ibn Umar, and other major companions. Scholars of hadith regarded him as fully reliable (thiqah). His narrations appear frequently in Bukhari and the other major collections, and he is counted as one of the important Tabi'i authorities in the Meccan scholarly tradition. He passed away around 117 AH.

Samurah ibn Jundab (RA) — this entry 'Samurah' is an alternate transliteration of the same companion documented under entry 50, appearing in Abu Dawud and Tirmidhi. He was a companion of the Prophet ﷺ who served as governor of Basra and narrated approximately 128 hadiths. His reports appear in the Sunan collections and include narrations on prayer, commercial dealings, and prophetic visions. He passed away around 58–59 AH.

Abu Mas'ud al-Ansari (RA) — this entry 'Abu Mas'ud Al-Ansari' is an alternate transliteration of the same companion documented under entry 51, Uqba ibn Amr al-Badri al-Ansari. He participated in the Second Pledge of Aqabah and earned the epithet 'al-Badri.' He narrated approximately 103 hadiths, settled in Kufa, and served as a religious authority there. His narrations appear in Bukhari and Tirmidhi. He passed away during the caliphate of Muawiya, around 40–42 AH.

Al-Miswar ibn Makhrama ibn Nawfal al-Zuhri al-Qurashi (RA) was born in Mecca approximately two years before the Hijra and came to Medina as a young child. He was the nephew of the companion Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf (RA), being his sister's son. He narrated from the Prophet ﷺ and from senior companions including Abu Bakr, Umar (RA), and others. He is particularly known for his narrations about the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, where he was present and observed the negotiations. His narrations on this event are among the most detailed accounts of that pivotal episode in Islamic history. He also narrated important hadiths on the prohibition of combining multiple wives within forbidden degrees. He was a resident of Medina and later died during the siege of Mecca by al-Hajjaj in approximately 64 AH.

Zaid ibn Arqam al-Ansari al-Khazraji (RA) was a young companion who participated in seventeen military expeditions with the Prophet ﷺ. He is known for one particularly important act of honesty: as a young man he overheard the leader of the hypocrites, Abdullah ibn Ubayy, making a statement of betrayal against the Prophet ﷺ. He reported it to the Prophet ﷺ despite pressure from his uncle to stay quiet. The subsequent revelation of Surah al-Munafiqun vindicated his report. He narrated approximately 66 hadiths. His famous narration of the Hadith of Ghadir Khumm — in which the Prophet ﷺ emphasized the importance of the Quran and his household — is one of his most cited narrations. He settled in Kufa and was held in high esteem. He passed away around 65–68 AH.

Sa'id ibn al-Musayyab ibn Hazn al-Makhzumi al-Qurashi was the foremost jurist of the Tabi'i generation and is universally regarded as the most learned of the Seven Jurists of Medina. His father and grandfather were both companions of the Prophet ﷺ. He learned hadith primarily from Abu Huraira (RA) — whose daughter he married — and also from Umar, Uthman, Ali, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, and Aisha (may Allah be pleased with them). He was distinguished above all others of his generation for the breadth of his legal scholarship and the depth of his piety. He consistently refused gifts from rulers and was imprisoned and flogged for refusing to pledge allegiance to al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik but maintained his principles throughout. He was the teacher of Imam al-Zuhri, Yahya ibn Sa'id al-Ansari, and other major scholars. He passed away around 94 AH.

Abu Sa'eed — this entry likely refers to Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (RA), the prolific Ansari companion documented under entries 11, 15, 17, 59, 79, and 86, appearing here in the Tirmidhi collection under a shortened form of his kunya. Sa'd ibn Malik ibn Sinan al-Ansari was one of the most devoted narrators of hadith after the Prophet's death, transmitting over 1,170 hadiths on all areas of Islamic practice. He passed away around 74 AH in Medina.

Abu Umamah al-Bahili (RA), whose full name was Sudayy ibn Ajlan ibn Wahb al-Bahili, was from the tribe of Bahila and embraced Islam during the Prophet's lifetime. He settled in Syria after the Prophet's death and became one of the most important companions in the Levant region. He was the last surviving companion in Syria and lived to around 86–88 AH — making him the final link in the entire companion generation in that important province. He narrated a significant number of hadiths — over 250 — and was particularly devoted to the recitation of Quran and the teaching of religion. He is known for hadiths encouraging the recitation of Surah al-Baqarah to protect the home from shaytan, and for numerous narrations on the virtues of various acts of worship. His narrations appear in Abu Dawud and other collections. This entry 'AbuUmamah' is an alternate transliteration.

Abu Musa al-Ashari (RA) — this entry 'AbuMusa al-Ash'ari' is an alternate transliteration of the same companion documented fully under entry 10. Abdullah ibn Qays al-Ashari was from Yemen, known for his beautiful Quran recitation, served as governor of Basra and Kufa, and narrated approximately 360 hadiths. He passed away around 42–52 AH.

Abu al-Darda' (RA), whose full name was Uwaymir ibn Malik ibn Zayd al-Ansari al-Khazraji, was from the Ansar of Medina. He embraced Islam after the Battle of Badr — reportedly having been the last of the Khazraj leaders to convert. Despite his late conversion he quickly distinguished himself through his extraordinary wisdom, deep piety, and asceticism. The Prophet ﷺ established a bond of brotherhood between him and Salman al-Farsi (RA), and Salman's famous counsel to Abu al-Darda' about balancing worship with the rights of the body became one of the most celebrated statements on moderation in Islamic tradition. He narrated approximately 179 hadiths. He was sent by Caliph Uthman to Damascus as a teacher and judge, where he remained until his death. He passed away around 32 AH. He is revered as one of the great ascetics and sages of the companion generation.

Asma bint Abi Bakr al-Siddiq (RA) was the elder sister of Aisha (RA) and the daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. She is known by the epithet Dhat al-Nitaqayn — the Lady of the Two Girdles — because she tore her girdle in two to tie the food provisions she carried to the Prophet ﷺ and her father as they hid in the Cave of Thawr during the Hijra. This act of courage and sacrifice at one of the most critical moments in Islamic history earned her enduring honour. She was married to al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam (RA) and was the mother of Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr. She narrated approximately 58 hadiths. She lived to 100 years of age — it is said her teeth never fell out and her mind remained sharp until the end. She passed away in 73 AH in Mecca, shortly after the martyrdom of her son Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr.

Abu Umamah al-Bahili (RA) — this entry 'Abu Umamah' is the same companion documented under entry 111, Sudayy ibn Ajlan al-Bahili, appearing in Tirmidhi, Abu Dawud, and Riyad al-Salihin under a slightly different transliteration. He was the last surviving companion in Syria, narrated over 250 hadiths, and passed away around 86–88 AH. His reports in Tirmidhi include important narrations on the virtues of worship and the Day of Judgment.

Al-Hasan al-Basri, whose full name was Abu Sa'id al-Hasan ibn Abi al-Hasan Yasar al-Basri, was one of the greatest Tabi'i scholars and spiritual figures in Islamic history. He was born in Medina in 21 AH, the son of a freed slave, and spent his early life in the household of Umm Salama (RA) — one of the Mothers of the Believers — which gave him access to the companion generation in the most intimate way. He narrated from many companions including Anas ibn Malik, Abu Huraira, and Aisha. He later settled in Basra and became its undisputed leading scholar in hadith, tafsir, legal opinion, and — above all — spiritual and moral guidance. His sermons and sayings on death, the afterlife, and the dangers of the worldly life are among the most powerful in Islamic literature. He was also a teacher of Tawus ibn Kaysan, Qatada ibn Di'ama, and many others. He passed away in Basra in 110 AH.

Uqbah ibn Amir al-Juhani (RA) — this entry 'Uqbah bin Amir' is an alternate transliteration of the same companion documented under entry 73, appearing in Tirmidhi and Abu Dawud. He was from the tribe of Juhayna, served as governor of Egypt under Muawiyah, was skilled in archery and Quranic recitation, and narrated approximately 55 hadiths. He passed away in Egypt around 58 AH.

Abu Bakra al-Thaqafi (RA) — various transliterations of this companion (Abu Bakra, AbuBakrah) all refer to the same person documented under entries 27 and 95. Nufay ibn al-Harith descended from the walls of Ta'if on a pulley to join the Muslims, was freed by the Prophet ﷺ, settled in Basra, and narrated approximately 132 hadiths. He is particularly known for the narration forbidding Muslims from fighting each other in civil strife. He passed away around 51–52 AH.

Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA) — this entry 'Abdullah bin Abbas' (without the RA marker) is the same companion documented under entries 5, 16, 21, and 96. He was the cousin of the Prophet ﷺ, the foremost Quranic exegete among the companions, and narrated over 1,660 hadiths. His knowledge was so vast that the companions themselves — despite being his seniors — sought his counsel on difficult questions of tafsir and law. He passed away around 68 AH in Ta'if.

Abu Ishaq al-Sabi'i — this entry is an alternate appearance of the same Tabi'i scholar documented under entry 85, Amr ibn Abdullah al-Hamdani al-Sabi'i of Kufa. He narrated from numerous companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib, al-Bara ibn Azib, and Zaid ibn Arqam, and was the teacher of Sufyan al-Thawri and Shu'ba. He was considered highly reliable in hadith criticism. He passed away around 127–129 AH in Kufa.
